content.text: Roles, RACI and Decision Rights for Claude Programs

Clarity about who does what in a Claude program prevents duplicated effort, missed responsibilities, and governance gaps. A RACI matrix (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) makes these assignments explicit across the use case lifecycle — from intake through incident response. Decision rights extend RACI by specifying not just who is involved, but who holds the authority to make each class of decision.

This document defines the key roles in a Claude program, walks through a reference RACI, and establishes a decision rights framework that scales from early-stage pilots to enterprise production deployments.
Core Role Definitions
Before building a RACI, each role must be precisely defined so that role names mean the same thing to everyone. Ambiguous role definitions produce ambiguous accountability.
Executive Sponsor

The most senior leader accountable for the Claude program's success. This role does not do delivery work — it holds budget authority, resolves cross-functional escalations, and provides organizational legitimacy. Typical titles: Chief AI Officer, CTO, Chief Digital Officer, or a senior VP with enterprise-wide mandate.
Key decision rights held by the Executive Sponsor:
Final approval on program budget and headcount
Resolution of executive-level escalations and cross-BU conflicts
Strategic direction changes that affect multiple departments
Public commitment to Claude deployment timelines
Sign-off on highest-risk use cases (e.g., customer-facing AI in regulated contexts)
CoE Lead

The operational leader of the Claude Center of Excellence. Accountable for CoE performance (SLA compliance, team capacity, catalog quality) and the primary stakeholder-facing role for day-to-day Claude program management. This is a full-time role requiring a blend of technical literacy, program management skill, and organizational influence.
Key decision rights held by the CoE Lead:
Approval of standard-risk use cases for production deployment
CoE service catalog additions and retirements
Resource allocation within CoE budget
SLA escalations and deadline extensions
Champion designation in the federated model
AI/ML Engineer (Claude Specialist)

The technical practitioner who designs, implements, and reviews Claude integrations. This role is responsible for the correctness and reliability of Claude-powered systems — prompt quality, API integration patterns, output evaluation, and performance optimization.
Key decision rights held by AI/ML Engineers:
Model selection (claude-haiku-4-5 vs. claude-sonnet-4-6 vs. claude-opus-4-8) for a given use case within defined cost parameters
Prompt design and revision for production systems
Integration architecture choices within approved security boundaries
Evaluation framework design for a specific use case
Recommendation (not approval) for production readiness
AI Governance / Responsible AI Analyst

Responsible for evaluating Claude use cases against the organization's AI risk framework and applicable regulations. This role produces the risk assessment artifact that gates production approval for higher-risk use cases.
Key decision rights held by the Governance Analyst:
Risk tier classification of a use case (low / medium / high risk)
Recommendation to approve or reject a use case at the governance gate
Policy maintenance: what changes to the acceptable use policy require executive sponsor sign-off vs. CoE Lead sign-off
Data classification decisions: what data can be sent to Claude APIs
Escalation to legal or compliance for use cases outside the standard framework
BU Champion

An embedded domain expert in a business unit who drives local Claude adoption in the federated model. Champions are not full-time CoE staff — they split 20–30% of their time on Claude-related work. They are the CoE's eyes and ears within their BU, the first point of contact for team members with Claude questions, and the primary delivery owner for BU-specific enablement.
Key decision rights held by BU Champions (within defined guardrails):
Approval of low-risk use cases within their BU without central CoE review
Training delivery decisions within their BU (topic, format, scheduling)
Prompt library contribution (champions can add domain-specific prompts to BU libraries without full CoE review)
Escalation trigger: champions decide when a use case exceeds their decision authority and must escalate to CoE
Product Owner / Business Requestor

The stakeholder who owns the business problem that Claude is being applied to solve. Product Owners define the use case, articulate success criteria, own the business case, and validate that Claude outputs meet their requirements. They are not responsible for the technical implementation but are accountable for the business outcome.
Reference RACI for the Claude Use Case Lifecycle

The following RACI covers six key decision points in a typical Claude use case lifecycle. 'R' (Responsible) means doing the work; 'A' (Accountable) means owning the outcome if it goes wrong; 'C' (Consulted) means input is sought before the decision; 'I' (Informed) means notified of the outcome.

attrs.alt: RACI matrix for Claude program decision rights across use case lifecycle

content.text: Key observations from this RACI:
Production approval has shared R:
both the CoE Lead and Governance Analyst must sign off — neither alone can approve a production deployment. This prevents one function from being a single point of failure.
Executive Sponsor holds A on risk and production:

the Executive Sponsor is not in the execution chain but is accountable for the outcomes of high-stakes decisions. This creates incentive for thoughtful risk assessment without slowing routine approvals.
BU Champion is R for intake:
champions are the primary intake channel in the federated model. The CoE Lead is accountable but delegates first-line triage to champions.
Incident response has three R's:

CoE Lead, AI Engineer, and Governance Analyst all have response responsibility. This reflects that incidents can be technical (prompt failure), operational (SLA breach), or compliance (data handling issue) in nature.
Expanding the RACI: Additional Decision Points
The reference RACI above covers the use case lifecycle. A complete enterprise RACI also covers program-level decisions:

Decision Rights Tiers

Beyond the RACI, a tiered decision rights framework clarifies who can approve what without escalation. This is especially important in the federated model, where BU champions need clear autonomy boundaries.

# Claude Program Decision Rights Tiers

Tier 1 — Self-service (no approval required)
  Who: Any authorized employee
  Examples:
    - Use the Claude.ai web interface for personal productivity
    - Access the internal prompt library
    - Attend a CoE training session
    - Experiment with Claude in a sandbox environment (non-production data only)

Tier 2 — Champion approval (BU Champion can approve)
  Who: BU Champions, within their business unit
  Examples:
    - Deploy a low-risk Claude use case (Tier 1 risk classification)
    - Add a domain-specific prompt to the BU prompt library
    - Run a lunch-and-learn without CoE facilitation
    - Integrate Claude into an internal tool with no customer-facing output
  Conditions: use case must score Tier 1 on risk assessment rubric;
              data classification must be Internal or lower

Tier 3 — CoE Lead approval (requires CoE review)
  Who: CoE Lead, after consultation with AI Engineer and Governance Analyst
  Examples:
    - Deploy a medium-risk Claude use case (Tier 2 risk classification)
    - New API integration into a production system
    - Customer-facing Claude integration with human-in-the-loop review
    - Non-standard model or configuration not in the approved list
  SLA: Decision within 10 business days of complete intake

Tier 4 — Executive Sponsor approval (requires committee review)
  Who: Executive Sponsor, after CoE Lead and Governance Analyst recommendation
  Examples:
    - High-risk use cases (Tier 3 risk classification)
    - Customer-facing AI with no human review in regulated domains
    - Autonomous Claude agent with consequential real-world actions
    - Use cases involving data classification above Internal
  SLA: Decision within 20 business days; may require legal or compliance sign-off
Documenting Decision Rights
A RACI and decision rights framework are only useful if they are documented in a place the organization actually reads. Best practices for maintaining this documentation:
Single source of truth:
maintain the RACI and decision rights tiers in one place — an internal wiki, Confluence page, or SharePoint site. Link to it from the CoE's intake form, service catalog, and onboarding guide.
Version-controlled:
document the date of last review and who approved changes. RACI documents that are never updated lose credibility quickly.
Role-indexed:
provide a role-specific view so a new BU Champion can look up 'what can I decide?' without reading the entire RACI matrix.
Decision log:
maintain a log of significant decisions made under Tier 3 and Tier 4, including the rationale. This builds institutional memory and helps the next person facing a similar decision.

# Decision log entry format
- date: 2026-03-15
  decision: Approved production deployment of Claude-powered contract review
  tier: 3
  decision_maker: CoE Lead (Jane Smith)
  consulted: [AI Engineer, Governance Analyst, Legal]
  use_case: Contract review assistant for procurement team
  risk_tier: 2 (medium — internal contracts, no customer data)
  rationale: >
    Use case involves internal documents only; human review required
    before any contract action; output is advisory not binding.
    Risk assessment completed 2026-03-10.
  conditions: >
    Quarterly review required; AI Engineer must monitor output
    quality for first 90 days; Governance Analyst notified of
    any escalation within 24 hours.
Common RACI Failure Modes
Even well-designed RACIs fail in practice if not actively maintained. Watch for:
Failure Mode
Symptom
Fix
Multiple Accountables
Two roles are both 'A' for the same decision — no one is clearly on the hook
Enforce the rule: exactly one A per row. If two roles share accountability, define how the tie is broken.
R without authority
A role is Responsible but lacks the resources or authority to execute
Pair R assignments with explicit decision rights and escalation paths
RACI not updated after reorg
Champion leaves; their successor doesn't know their decision rights
Tie RACI review to org changes; version-control the document
Consulted overload
Too many C's on every decision — every meeting has 10 people
Distinguish C (input shapes the decision) from I (informed after). Most I's are misclassified C's.
Ignored in practice
Teams make decisions without following the RACI
Surface RACI in intake form; CoE Lead enforces at governance gate; celebrate correct use publicly
RACI for Agentic and Autonomous Claude Use Cases

As Claude programs mature, teams begin building agentic systems — Claude instances that take sequences of actions with real-world consequences (sending emails, writing files, calling external APIs). These use cases require extended decision rights clarity because errors are harder to reverse.
Accountable role must be human:
autonomous Claude agents cannot hold accountability in any governance framework. A specific human role must be accountable for each agentic system's behavior.
Incident response R expands:
agentic systems can fail in novel ways (unexpected action sequences, unintended side effects). The incident response RACI should include the Platform/SRE team for agentic use cases alongside the CoE.
Production approval requires Tier 4 review:
agentic use cases with consequential real-world actions should require Executive Sponsor sign-off regardless of risk tier classification, given the novelty and potential blast radius.
Human-in-the-loop requirements should be explicit:
the RACI should document at what points a human must review and approve before the agent proceeds. This becomes part of the production approval criteria.
Onboarding New Roles to the RACI

A RACI is only effective if everyone who appears in it understands their assignments. New CoE team members, new BU champions, and new executive sponsors all need RACI onboarding as part of their role induction.
For New CoE Team Members
Walk through the full RACI in the first week, focusing on where they hold R vs. where they are C or I
Shadow at least one intake triage, one risk assessment, and one production approval in the first month
Understand the escalation path: which decisions can they make alone, which require CoE Lead, which require Executive Sponsor?
Review the decision log to understand how past Tier 3 and Tier 4 decisions were reasoned through

# New CoE Team Member RACI Onboarding Checklist

Week 1:
  ☐ Review full RACI matrix and decision rights tiers document
  ☐ 1:1 with CoE Lead to discuss role-specific decision rights
  ☐ Read last 5 entries in the decision log

Week 2-4:
  ☐ Shadow one intake triage session
  ☐ Shadow one risk assessment review
  ☐ Shadow one production approval decision
  ☐ Review 2-3 past use case files (intake → risk → architecture → approval)

Month 2:
  ☐ Lead first intake triage with CoE Lead observing
  ☐ Lead first risk assessment (if governance role) or architecture review (if engineer role)
  ☐ Debrief with CoE Lead on any RACI ambiguities encountered
For New BU Champions
Provide a champion-specific RACI view that focuses only on their decision rights (Tier 1 and Tier 2 decisions)
Make explicit what they can approve without escalation vs. what must go to CoE Lead
Walk through the escalation trigger: 'If you see X, escalate immediately'
Connect them with existing champions for peer knowledge sharing
Schedule a 90-day check-in to revisit any RACI questions that emerged in practice

# BU Champion RACI Quick Reference Card

You CAN approve without CoE review:
  ✓ Tier 1 risk use cases (see risk rubric)
  ✓ BU-internal tools with no customer-facing output
  ✓ Data classification: Public or Internal only
  ✓ Prompt additions to BU prompt library

You MUST escalate to CoE Lead:
  → Tier 2 or higher risk classification
  → Any customer-facing Claude output
  → Data classification: Confidential or above
  → Integration with third-party systems
  → Any use case with autonomous actions

Escalation contact: [CoE Lead name] at [contact]
Emergency (incident): [CoE Lead] + [AI Engineer] within 1 hour
For New Executive Sponsors
The Executive Sponsor's RACI role is primarily about what comes to them for approval, not day-to-day involvement
Key orientation: understand the four tiers, especially what qualifies as Tier 4 that requires their sign-off
Understand the escalation path so they know when to expect CoE Lead to bring them a decision vs. when decisions are made below their level
Agree on communication preferences: how often does the Executive Sponsor want CoE updates, and in what format?
Adapting the RACI for Small Organizations

Not every organization can staff all six roles distinctly. Smaller CoEs often need to consolidate roles, which is acceptable as long as accountability remains clear and certain role combinations are avoided.
Acceptable Combinations
Avoid Combining
CoE Lead + Program Manager (1 person coordinates and manages)
CoE Lead + Governance Analyst (approval and risk assessment should be independent)
AI Engineer + BU Champion (in small BUs, the engineer can champion their own team)
Executive Sponsor + CoE Lead (the person who does the work cannot also hold final accountability for budget)
Governance Analyst + Policy Writer (same person develops and reviews policy)
AI Engineer + Governance Analyst (technical implementation and risk review benefit from independence)
When combining roles, always ask: 'If this person makes a mistake, who notices?' If the answer is 'nobody in the chain,' the combination introduces a blind spot that needs a compensating control.
Using the RACI in Performance Management
RACI assignments can be mapped to performance expectations, making organizational accountability visible in annual reviews and role definitions:
R (Responsible):
delivery commitments in individual performance plans. If a CoE AI Engineer is R for 'Prompt and eval quality,' their targets should include measurable quality outcomes.
A (Accountable):
outcomes that the role is measured on at a leadership level. The CoE Lead's performance includes SLA compliance, stakeholder satisfaction, and governance gate adherence — all areas where they hold A.
C (Consulted):
reflects the role's need to be kept in the loop on decisions that affect their work. Performance plans for consulted roles should include 'participates effectively in CoE governance processes.'
Absence from RACI:

roles not appearing in the RACI for a given decision should not be pulled into meetings about it. One practical benefit of a published RACI is reducing unnecessary meeting load by making it clear who actually needs to be present.
Summary
Clear roles, an explicit RACI, and a tiered decision rights framework prevent the accountability gaps and bottlenecks that derail Claude programs at scale.
Define six core roles: Executive Sponsor, CoE Lead, AI Engineer, Governance Analyst, BU Champion, Product Owner
Each role holds specific decision rights — document them alongside the RACI to prevent ambiguity
Build a RACI for six key lifecycle points: intake, risk assessment, architecture, production approval, quality, incident response
Layer the RACI with a four-tier decision rights framework: self-service, champion approval, CoE Lead approval, Executive Sponsor approval
Exactly one Accountable per decision — enforce this rule to ensure clear ownership when things go wrong
Maintain a decision log for Tier 3+ decisions to build institutional memory
Agentic use cases require extended accountability clarity — humans must own the outcome of autonomous Claude systems
